verdict: 2026-07-03 07:04:50 PDT — The core design is sound: pinning `{ authTagLength: 16 }` on the two first-party AES-GCM decrypt sites is the right hardening for the semgrep finding, and the repo sweep found no other first-party GCM decrypt or CCM sites. It is backward-compatible with the current envelopes: both encrypt sides use Node's default `getAuthTag()` length of 16 bytes, and the Zoom app-context layout already treats the trailing tag as 16 bytes. No pre-`setAuthTag` length check is needed because the pinned decipher option makes Node reject non-16-byte tags before authentication proceeds. The only gap is in the concrete test sketch: it should exercise wrong-length tags, not only same-length tag tampering.

findings.title: Test plan does not exercise the tag-length invariant directly
findings.claim: The concrete AC3 test notes call for flipping a tag byte in `rateCrypto` and mangling the trailing tag byte in `appContext`. Those are valid integrity tests, but they do not directly cover the semgrep issue: accepting a shorter-than-expected GCM tag. A same-length tag flip would already fail before this hardening, so the tests as sketched leave the new 16-byte length invariant implicit.
findings.alternative: Add explicit wrong-length cases at both boundaries: for `rateCrypto`, truncate or extend `blob.tag` after `encrypt` and assert `decrypt` throws; for `appContext`, decode the context, remove or append a trailing tag byte, re-encode, and assert `resolveUid` throws `AppContextError`. Keep the same-length tag-flip tests as ordinary tamper coverage.
findings.win: The tests then document and lock the exact security invariant being hardened: valid 16-byte existing envelopes still decrypt, while non-16-byte auth tags are rejected at both auth boundaries.
